title: GSoC’26 Reports
text:
Building FloraFit – My Journey into Flower Circuit Analysis


How a discussion with my mentor introduced me to Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy, eventually leading to the development of FloraFit and our…



Jun 8, 2026













Community Bonding Period – A Month of Learning Beyond Code


My experience and explorations with hyperSpec and R during the community bonding period.



May 24, 2026






No matching items

title: Building FloraFit – My Journey into Flower Circuit Analysis
text:
One of the things I enjoy most about research is that you never quite know where a conversation will lead.
A few weeks ago, my mentor introduced me to an area I had never explored before—Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS). At first, it sounded like something far removed from software engineering. The idea of studying flower petals using electrical measurements wasn’t something I had ever imagined.
As we discussed the research further, I learned that flower petals have surprisingly rich electrical behaviour. By applying a small alternating current across a petal and measuring how it responds at different frequencies, researchers can gain insights into its internal structure and physiological state.
The research itself was fascinating, but there was another challenge.
Once researchers collect impedance data, they need to fit it to an equivalent electrical circuit to understand what the measurements actually mean. Existing tools weren’t designed specifically for flowers, and much of the analysis involved manual work.
That’s when we decided to build FloraFit.



Before contributing to FloraFit, I knew very little about Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy.
The first few days weren’t spent writing code—they were spent reading.
I learned how biological tissues conduct electricity, why they cannot simply be represented as a resistor, and how different structures inside a flower contribute to the overall electrical response.
One concept that immediately stood out to me was equivalent circuit modelling.
Instead of analysing thousands of impedance measurements directly, researchers build electrical circuits using familiar components like resistors and capacitors. If the circuit behaves like the flower, then its electrical parameters can reveal useful biological information.
It was an elegant way of connecting biology and electrical engineering.

📷 Picture: Insert a simple diagram explaining EIS or a flower impedance measurement setup.





As I understood the research better, we started discussing how we could make the analysis easier for researchers.
Rather than requiring users to manually fit circuit parameters, we wanted software that could perform the fitting automatically while remaining easy to use.
That idea eventually became FloraFit.
We built FloraFit as an interactive R Shiny application that takes impedance measurements as input, fits different equivalent circuit models, and visualizes the results through Nyquist and Bode plots. Alongside the plots, the application also reports the estimated circuit parameters and fitting statistics, making it easier to compare models and interpret experiments. :contentReferenceoaicite:0
One thing I appreciated while working on the application was how modular everything was. Circuit models, optimization routines, plotting, and the user interface were all separated cleanly, making it straightforward to extend the software as new models are developed. :contentReferenceoaicite:1

📷 Picture: Screenshot of the FloraFit interface (Figure 5 from the paper).





One thing I found particularly interesting was that there isn’t a single electrical circuit that can represent every biological tissue.
Different models make different assumptions about how electricity travels through the tissue.
FloraFit currently supports four commonly used equivalent circuit models:

Voigt Model, which represents the tissue using multiple resistor-capacitor branches.
Single-Shell Model, where each cell is represented by a conductive interior surrounded by a membrane.
Double-Shell Model, which captures more complex cellular structures by introducing additional compartments.
Cole Model, which models impedance using a compact mathematical representation based on frequency-dependent dispersion.

Each model provides a different balance between biological realism and mathematical simplicity, allowing researchers to choose the one that best describes their experimental data. :contentReferenceoaicite:2
What I liked most was seeing how electrical engineering concepts could be used to describe living biological systems.

📷 Picture: Figure showing the four equivalent circuit models (Figure 1).





Initially, I assumed fitting would simply involve finding the parameters that minimized an error.
It turned out to be much more challenging.
Equivalent circuit models often contain several parameters, and different parameter combinations can produce remarkably similar impedance curves. Starting from poor initial values can easily cause optimization algorithms to converge to incorrect solutions.
To make the fitting more reliable, FloraFit first estimates sensible initial parameters before performing multi-stage nonlinear optimization. This greatly improves convergence while keeping the estimated parameters physically meaningful. :contentReferenceoaicite:3
Once the optimization is complete, the software generates Nyquist and Bode plots that allow users to visually compare the fitted model against the measured data.
Watching the measured points gradually align with the fitted curve was one of those moments where all the mathematics finally felt tangible.

📷 Picture: Nyquist plot (Figure 3).


📷 Picture: Bode magnitude and phase plots (Figure 4).





As FloraFit continued to mature, we also began documenting the software as a SoftwareX paper.
This was my first experience contributing to a paper focused entirely on scientific software.
Writing the paper was very different from writing code.
Instead of thinking about implementation details, we had to explain the motivation behind the software, describe the fitting methodology clearly, organize figures, and demonstrate why the application is useful for researchers.
Seeing the project evolve from research discussions into a working application—and now into a paper that we are preparing to submit—has been incredibly rewarding. :contentReferenceoaicite:4

📷 Picture: Screenshot of the paper’s title page or a photo of you working on the manuscript (if you have one).





When my mentor first introduced me to Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy, I expected to simply learn about a new scientific technique.
I didn’t expect that conversation to lead to building a research application and co-authoring a scientific paper.
Working on FloraFit taught me much more than how to build an R Shiny application.
It introduced me to plant electrophysiology, numerical optimization, equivalent circuit modelling, and the process of developing software that directly supports scientific research.
Perhaps the biggest lesson I learned is that good research software isn’t just about writing code.
It’s about making complex scientific methods easier to use, more reproducible, and more accessible to the people who need them.
I’m excited to see where FloraFit goes next, and I look forward to seeing researchers use it to better understand the fascinating electrical behaviour of flowers.

title: Community Bonding Period – A Month of Learning Beyond Code
text:
When people think of Google Summer of Code, they often imagine the coding period—pull requests, implementations, and long debugging sessions. Surprisingly, my Community Bonding period turned out to be just as valuable, even though very little of it involved writing the code that my proposal was centered around.
My proposal focused on building a high-performance Rust backend for graph-based smoothing of hyperspectral images in the hyperSpec ecosystem, bridging R’s sparse matrices with Rust to accelerate spectral processing. The weeks before coding officially began became an opportunity to broaden my understanding of spectroscopy, improve my R skills, collaborate with my mentor, and even contribute to projects outside my proposal.
Looking back, it felt less like preparation and more like becoming part of the community.



One of my goals during this period was to become more comfortable with the R ecosystem surrounding spectroscopy.
I spent a significant amount of time exploring hyperSpec and related packages, understanding how spectral datasets are represented, manipulated, and visualized. While my proposal is heavily focused on performance through Rust, I wanted to understand how researchers actually interact with spectral data from the R side before thinking about optimization. Reading package documentation gradually turned into experimenting with real datasets, trying different preprocessing techniques, and understanding why existing workflows were designed the way they are.
It reminded me that optimization only makes sense once you truly understand the problem you’re trying to accelerate.




One of the best parts of the bonding period was getting to know my mentor better. Our meetings certainly involved discussions about the project, timelines, implementation ideas, and research papers, but they were never limited to those topics. We ended up talking about programming languages, open source, scientific software, systems programming, academia, and sometimes completely unrelated ideas that simply happened to come up. Those conversations made the mentorship feel much more personal than transactional.
Instead of only discussing what should be built, we often talked about why certain design choices mattered, how scientific software evolves over time, and what makes research code maintainable for years.
As someone who enjoys understanding systems from first principles, these discussions were easily one of my favorite parts of the month.




Although my primary GSoC project hadn’t officially started, I found myself contributing to another project called FloraFit.
FloraFit is a tool that automatically fits equivalent electrical circuit models to electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) data collected from flower petals. Rather than manually selecting parameters, the software estimates the circuit components that best describe the measured impedance response.
Working on FloraFit exposed me to an entirely different side of spectroscopy. Instead of hyperspectral imaging, I was now thinking about electrical models, impedance spectra, equivalent circuits, optimization, and model fitting.
It was fascinating to see how similar mathematical ideas appear across completely different scientific domains.
Beyond the implementation itself, it also reinforced something I enjoy about research software—writing code that directly helps scientists interpret experimental data.




Alongside the software itself, I also had the opportunity to contribute to the accompanying scientific paper.
This was a completely different experience compared to programming.
Writing a paper requires thinking carefully about how to explain ideas rather than simply implementing them. Every figure, every equation, and every paragraph has to tell a coherent story.
I worked on describing the methodology, refining explanations, improving diagrams, and making the technical content easier to follow.
It gave me a much greater appreciation for the amount of effort that goes into communicating research effectively.




Another memorable experience during the Community Bonding period was attending an R Stats community meeting, where I was introduced to Quarto.
Until then, most of my documentation had been written in either Markdown or LaTeX. Quarto immediately stood out because of how naturally it combines technical writing, code, figures, and website generation into a single workflow. The session walked us through creating blog posts, organizing content, and generating a complete static website, making it much easier to share technical work in a clean and reproducible way.
Inspired by the session, I decided to build my own blog website.
What started as a small experiment quickly turned into something I genuinely enjoyed working on. Setting up the website gave me an opportunity to learn more about Quarto’s features while creating a space where I could document my GSoC journey, write about interesting technical problems, and share things I learn throughout the summer.
I also realized that open source isn’t just about writing good code—it’s equally about communicating ideas. Having a personal blog encourages me to document my work more thoughtfully, and I hope it will serve as a useful record of both my successes and the lessons I learn along the way.




When I first looked at the Community Bonding timeline in my proposal, I imagined it would mostly involve setting up repositories, reading documentation, and preparing for coding. :contentReferenceoaicite:1 Instead, it became a month filled with learning.
I explored spectral programming in R, learned from discussions with my mentor, contributed to FloraFit, worked on a scientific paper, discovered Quarto through the R community, and built a blog that you’re reading now. None of these activities directly completed a project milestone, but together they built the foundation for everything that follows during the coding period.
Sometimes the most valuable progress isn’t measured by the number of commits you make—it’s measured by how much your perspective grows.

title: About
text: This is my blog for GSoC 2026 under The R Project for Statistical Computing.
